items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.

items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Which R is best to do first?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reduce. “Recycle” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: A waste burial site. “A factory” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many Rs are in the classic 'reduce, reuse, recycle' rule?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
